title: DAY 1: Getting Started – Your Editing Environment & First Cut
objective: By the end of Day 1, you will have editing software installed, understand the interface, and make your very first simple edit.

detailedContent.title: Choosing Your Editing Software
detailedContent.content:
The best editing software is the one you'll actually use. 

**Free Options (Highly Recommended to Start):**
- **DaVinci Resolve:** Windows/Mac. Professional standard for color.
- **CapCut:** Windows/Mac/Mobile. Easiest for social media content.
- **iMovie:** Mac only. Simple and intuitive.

**Common Interface Elements:**
1. **Media Bin:** Where you store footage.
2. **Preview Monitor:** Watch clips before timeline.
3. **Timeline:** Where you build your story.
4. **Toolbar:** Tools for cutting and moving.

detailedContent.title: Your First Edit – The Three‑Clip Story
detailedContent.content:
You don't need fancy effects to tell a story. Today you'll edit three simple clips in order to show a simple action: someone walking into a room, sitting down, and picking up a book.

**Key Terms:**
- **Clip:** A single piece of footage.
- **Sequence:** Your project timeline.
- **Track:** Layers in the timeline.
- **Export:** Creating the final video file.

video.title: How to Edit Videos for Absolute Beginners
video.description: Think Media covers the fundamental concepts that apply to any editing software.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Software Setup & Exploration (15 minutes)
steps: Install DaVinci Resolve or CapCut
Open and locate the media bin, timeline, and export buttons

exercises.title: Exercise 2: The Three-Clip Edit (20 minutes)
steps: Shoot or download 3 clips: walking to door, sitting, picking up a book
Import and name clearly (walk_in, sit_down, pick_book)
Drag to timeline in order and trim the ends for clean transitions

exercises.title: Exercise 3: Export Your First Video (5 minutes)
steps: Export as H.264/MP4 and watch your 10-second story

reflectionPrompt: How did it feel to make my first edit? What part of the software felt confusing?

title: DAY 2: The Art of the Cut – Techniques & Pacing
objective: By the end of Day 2, you will understand different types of cuts and how to use them to control pacing and emotion.

detailedContent.title: Cuts Are the Language of Film
detailedContent.content:
In editing, cuts are punctuation. 

**Essential Cut Types:**
1. **Standard Cut:** Switching from one clip to next.
2. **Jump Cut:** Removing a middle section (good for vlogs).
3. **J‑Cut:** Audio from next clip starts *before* video cuts.
4. **L‑Cut:** Video from previous clip continues while audio from next starts.
5. **Cutaway:** Cutting to a detail shot before cutting back.

**Pacing:** How long each shot stays on screen. Fast = energy/chaos; Slow = calm/reflection.

video.title: 7 Types of Cuts Every Video Editor Should Know
video.description: StudioBinder explains cinematic cuts with great visual examples.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Dialogue Practice (15 minutes)
steps: Import a dialogue clip of two people talking
Practice standard cuts between the speakers

exercises.title: Exercise 2: Mastering J-Cuts & L-Cuts (15 minutes)
steps: Create a J-cut so B starts speaking while we still see A
Create an L-cut to show B's reaction while A is still speaking

exercises.title: Exercise 3: Pacing Challenge (5 minutes)
steps: Edit a 5-clip sequence in two versions: 1 second per shot (fast) and 4 seconds per shot (slow)
Observe how the emotion changes

reflectionPrompt: Which cut type feels most useful for my projects? What did I notice about pacing?

title: DAY 3: Audio Editing – The 50% of Your Video
objective: By the end of Day 3, you will be able to clean up audio, balance levels, add music, and use sound effects.

detailedContent.title: Audio Makes or Breaks Your Video
detailedContent.content:
BAD audio ruins GOOD video. 

**The Three Audio Layers:**
1. **Dialogue:** Most important. Must be clear.
2. **Music:** Sets emotion.
3. **SFX:** Adds realism.

**Basic Audio Workflow:**
- **Set levels:** Dialogue at -12dB to -6dB. Music lower (-20dB).
- **Keyframing:** Use to fade in/out or 'duck' music under voices.
- **Noise Reduction:** Remove hum and hiss.

video.title: Audio Editing for Video Beginners – Complete Guide
video.description: Learn to manage audio layers and professional mixing techniques.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Set Dialogue Levels (10 minutes)
steps: Import dialogue and adjust volume to hit -12dB to -6dB peaks

exercises.title: Exercise 2: Music & Ducking (20 minutes)
steps: Add music track and lower its baseline volume
Use keyframes to duck music by ~15dB whenever dialogue starts

exercises.title: Exercise 3: SFX & Noise Cleanup (10 minutes)
steps: Add 2-3 sound effects to support visual actions
Apply noise reduction to dialogue if environment was noisy

reflectionPrompt: What was hardest about audio editing? How did music change the feel of the video?

title: DAY 4: Transitions & Effects – Purposeful Movement
objective: By the end of Day 4, you will understand how to use transitions and effects to support the story.

detailedContent.title: Less Is Often More
detailedContent.content:
Beginners love flashy transitions; pros use them sparingly. 

**Common Transitions:**
- **Cut:** 90% of edits. Invisible.
- **Cross Dissolve:** Passage of time / montages.
- **Dip to Black:** End of scene / significant time jump.
- **Fade In/Out:** Start/End of video.

**Video Effects:**
- **Stabilization:** Fix shaky footage.
- **Speed Ramping:** Dramatic slow or fast motion within a clip.

video.title: When to Use Transitions in Video Editing
video.description: Explores the storytelling psychology behind every transition.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Basic Transitions (10 minutes)
steps: Edit a 4-clip sequence using cross dissolves and dip-to-black
Compare the feeling of a cut vs. a dissolve

exercises.title: Exercise 2: Technical Fixes (10 minutes)
steps: Apply stabilization to a shaky clip
Practice speed ramping: go from fast to slow motion smoothly

exercises.title: Exercise 3: The Mini-Montage (10 minutes)
steps: Create a 15-second montage set to music using purposeful transitions

reflectionPrompt: How did speed changes affect the emotion of my clips?

title: DAY 5: Color Correction & Grading
objective: By the end of Day 5, you will be able to correct technical color issues and apply creative grades.

detailedContent.title: Color Tells the Story
detailedContent.content:
1. **Color Correction:** Technical fix for exposure and white balance.
2. **Color Grading:** Creative manipulation to set a specific mood.

**Key Concepts:**
- **Exposure:** Overall brightness.
- **Contrast:** Punchiness of the image.
- **White Balance:** Removing unwanted color tints.
- **Lift/Gamma/Gain:** Shadows, midtones, highlights.

video.title: Color Correction vs. Color Grading – Beginner's Guide
video.description: DaVinci Resolve-based workflow that applies to any color tool.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Basic Color Correction (15 minutes)
steps: Fix a clip that is too dark (Exposure)
Fix a clip that looks too yellow (White Balance)
Increase contrast on a 'flat' clip

exercises.title: Exercise 2: Creative Grading (15 minutes)
steps: Push a clip toward a 'Warm' look (yellow/orange highlights)
Push a clip toward a 'Cool/Cinematic' look (teal/blue shadows)
Apply a pre-made LUT and adjust its strength

reflectionPrompt: What surprised me about color correction? Which creative grade felt most impactful?

title: DAY 6: Text, Titles, & Motion Graphics
objective: By the end of Day 6, you will be able to add professional graphics and captions to your videos.

detailedContent.title: Text as a Visual Tool
detailedContent.content:
**Types of Text:**
1. **Titles:** Opening credits.
2. **Lower Thirds:** Speaker names/titles.
3. **Captions:** Subtitles for accessibility.

**Legibility Tips:**
- Match font to mood.
- Use high contrast (outline or drop shadow).
- Position in 'safe zones' (avoid edges/faces).

video.title: How to Add Text & Titles in [Your Software]
video.description: Learn to style and animate text for professional results.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: Branding Your Video (15 minutes)
steps: Create a centered title with a fade animation
Create a lower-third for a speaker with a slide-in animation
Add basic captions to a 10-second speech segment

exercises.title: Exercise 2: Keyframe Animation (15 minutes)
steps: Animate a text box to grow from 0 to 100% scale
Animate a title flying in from the side using position keyframes

reflectionPrompt: How important do captions seem for social media? What graphic do I want to learn next?

title: DAY 7: Final Project – Your First Masterpiece
objective: By the end of Day 7, you will have planed, edited, and exported a complete 60‑second video.

detailedContent.title: The Final Assembly
detailedContent.content:
**Project Requirements:**
- Multiple clips with J/L cuts.
- Music with ducking.
- Purposeful transitions.
- Consistent color correction.
- Title, lower-third, and end screen.
- Exported at 1080p H.264.

video.title: How I Edit a Video from Start to Finish
video.description: A comprehensive look at the professional editing workflow in action.

exercises.title: Exercise 1: The Final Project (60+ minutes)
steps: Plan a 60-second video: vlog, tutorial, or montage
Import and Rough Cut your best clips
Refine with J/L cuts and Audio ducking
Apply Color Correction and Text Graphics
Export and watch your final 7-day milestone!

reflectionPrompt: Compare this to your Day 1 edit. What are the three biggest improvements?
